content.full_text:
Re:
Fern Polrrrcel PRAcrrcps CoMMrssroN
.128 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95811-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
August 6,2009
Phaedra A. Norton
Turlock City Attorney
City of Turlock
156 South Broadway, Suite 240
Turlock, California 95380-5454
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-09-173
Dear Ms. Norton:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Because you seek general
information, we are providing informal assistance. lnformal assistance does not provide
the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) Please also note that our informal assistance is
based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the
application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of
interest, or any other laws that apply to Mr. Salcedo's situation.
QUESTTON
May Mr. Salcedo, a city planning commissioner, participate in planning
commission decisions to approve or disapprove potential development projects if he is
employed by Teichert Construction, a construction company that may bid on projects that
the commission has approved?
CONCLUSION
The commissioner may participate in a planning commission decision regarding a
development project, unless the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on his employer.
'ThePoliticalReformActiscontainedinGovernmentCodeSecrions8l000throughgl014. 
All
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory ret'erences are.to Title 2, Division 6 of the Califbrnia Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. l-09-173
Page No. 2
FACTS
Alex Salcedo, a planning commissioner for the city of Turlock, has asked you to
inquire on his behalf regarding the conflict-of-interest prwisions of the.Act. As a
planning commissioner, he will be called upon to maki or participate in making decisions
to approve or disapprove potential construction projects. Mr. Saliedo is also employed
as a superintendant by Teichert Construction ("Teichert"). His job responsibilities
include the oversight and management of concrete and underground construction.
Teichert is a privately owned construction company that may bid on projects that have
been approved by the planning commission.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will ..perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section gl00l(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official fronr-making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influenc" a gor".n-ental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation tSTOO(U).) The general
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists wheniver a public official *ui.", u
govemmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable matirial financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests.
1. Is Mr. Salcedo a "public official" within the meaning of section 87100?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local govemment agency." As a member of the city's'planning
commission, which is a local government agency, Mr. Salcedo is a publi" offi.iut.
Therefore, he may not make, participate in making, or otherwis" ur" hi, position to
influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material iinancial effect
on any of his economic interests.
2. will Mr. salcedo be making, participating in making or influencing a
governmental decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contrictual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the auihority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmakeiregarding the govemmental


File No. l-09-173
Page No. 3
decision. (Regulation 1,8702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)
By participating in a planning commission vote to approve or disapprove a
potential development project, Mr. Salcedo will be making a governmentiidecision.
3. What are Mr. Salcedo,s economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in
Section 87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5, inclusive:
o d public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of 92,000 or more. (Section AitOg(a);
Regulation I 8703. 1(a).)
o d public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)
o A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section STtO](bj; Regulation
18703.2.)
o An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(c); Regulation I 8703.3.)
o { public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section g7103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. tnir i.
commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section g7i03;
Regulation 18703.5.)
Business Entity & Source of Income
You have disclosed only Mr. Salcedo's interest in Teichert. Thus, we limit our
analysis to that interest. Mr. Salcedo has an economic interest in Teichert because he
holds a management position in it. (Section 87103(d); Regularion 1g703.1(b).)


File No. t-09-173
Page No.4
Presumably, Teichert is also a source of income of $500 or more. (Section g7103(c);
Regulation 18703.3.)
Personal Ftnances
A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances.
(Regulation 18703.5.) A governmental decision will have an effect on tt i. economic
interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of
the official, or his or her immediate family, increasing or decreasing. (Ibid.)
4. Will Mr. Salcedo's economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in
decisions he will make, participate in making or influence as a public ofhciat?
Regulation 18704.1provides that a person, including business entities and sources
of income, is directly involved in a decision before an officLl's agency when that person,
either directly or indirectly or by an agent, either initiates the proc-eeding in which the
decision will be made, is-a named party,-or is the subject of thi proceedi"ng concerning
the decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the suiject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuanc", ."n"*al, approval, denial or"revocation of
any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.
(Regul ation 187 0a J @)(Z).)
Decisions to approve or disapprove a potential development project are typically
decisions of a general nature, not involving any particular party or partils. Under these
circumstances, it does not appear that Teicherrwill be initiating, nor will it be a named
party in or the subject of, a proceeding regarding a proposed dJvelopment proj;";.- -
Accordingly, Teichert will probably not be directlylnvolved in this govern*"rtut
decision. (Regulation 18704.1.). If a public official's economic inteiest is not directly
involved in a governmental decision, it is indirectly involved. (Regulation lg70a(a).)
5. What is the applicable materiality standard?
Business Entities and Sources of Income
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) Regulation 18705.1(c) sets forth the materiality standard s for
indirectly involved economic interests in business entities, including those that are
sources of income. The regulation applies a number of standards, tiilored to the size of
the business entity in which the public official has an economic interest. The standards
are expressed in terms of dollar impact over a period of time on the business entity's
"gross revenues," "expenses," and ..assets or liabilities.
For example, for a company with earnings before taxes of no less than $750,000
in its most recent fiscal year, the governmental decision would have a material effect on


File No. l-09-173
Page No. 5
the company if the decision will increase or decrease the company's gross revenues in a
fiscal year by $300,000 or more, will cause the busines s to tncur or aioid additionat
expenses or reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year by $100,000 or more,
or will increase or decrease the value of its assets or liabilitiei Uy $iOO,oOo. Because
you have provided no information regarding Teichert's finances, we have prepared the
table below to assist you in determining the applicable materiality standa.d f- a privately
held company under Regulation 18705.1(c).
Personal Finances
A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's or his or her
immediate family's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any l2-month
period.
6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision on Mr. Salcedo's economic interests will meet the applicablf materiality
standard?
An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially
likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) I Fppc ops. l9g.) whether rhe
financial consequences of a govemmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the specific facts surrounding the decision. '(ln re Thorner,
supra.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered r"urorubly
foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable.
The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops.72.) Therefore, the determination of whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for
Mr. Salcedo's economic interest is necessarily a faciual question that is ultimately for
him to decide. As to Mr. Salcedo's interest in Teichert, he must determine, at the time he
is called upon to make a govemmental decision, whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financiar effect on Teichert.
Type of Business
Gross Revenues
Expenses
Assets/Liabilities
Revenues of no less than
the company listed as
500D on the Fortune 500
$10,000,000 or more
$2,500,000 or more
$10,000,000 or more
Earnings before taxes of
no less than $2,500,000
$s00,000
$200,000
$s00,000
Earnings before taxes of
no less than $750,000
$300,000
$ r00,000
$300,000
All others
$20,000
$s,000
$20,000


File No. l-09-173
Page No. 6
7 & 8. The "public generally" and "legally required participation',
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
govemmental decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
its effect on the public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 1g700(b)(7) and lg7ol(a)),
or if the official is legally required to participate (Se-tion 87103; Regulation lg70g).
You have not presented any facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable
to the commissioner's situation.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
exceptions.
vJjgl
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

employed by Teichert Construction, a construction company that may bid on projects that
the commission has approved?

development project, unless the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on his employer.

commission decisions to approve or disapprove potential development projects if he is
employed by Teichert Construction, a construction company that may bid on projects that
the commission has approved?

CONCLUSION: The commissioner may participate in a planning commission decision regarding a
development project, unless the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on his employer.
